content.problemStatement:
There are NNN people lost in a forest, and each person has a radio. The iii-th person's radio can transmit KiK_iKi​ frequencies. Let the list of frequencies that the iii-th person can transmit be Fi=[f(i,1),f(i,2),…,f(i,Ki)]F_i = [f_{(i,1)}, f_{(i,2)}, \ldots, f_{(i, K_i)}]Fi​=[f(i,1)​,f(i,2)​,…,f(i,Ki​)​].

The iii-th person and jjj-th person can communicate with each other if **at least** one of the following holds true:


They can both transmit a common frequency i.e. (f(i,a)=f(j,b)f_{(i, a)} = f_{(j, b)}f(i,a)​=f(j,b)​ for some a,ba, ba,b).
There exists a person kkk who can communicate with both iii-th person and jjj-th person.
You want to ensure that every pair of people can communicate with each other. To do so, you can perform the following operation:


You can transfer a frequency from one person's radio to another, i.e. select iii and jjj (1≤i,j≤N)(1 \le i, j \le N)(1≤i,j≤N),
delete a frequency xxx (present in FiF_iFi​) from FiF_iFi​, and append it to FjF_jFj​.
Determine the **minimum** number of operations to ensure that every pair of people can communicate with each other. If it is not possible, print −1-1−1.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the number of people
The next NNN lines describe the list of frequencies for each person.
The iii-th of these NNN lines contains an integer KiK_iKi​, the number of frequencies the radio of the iii-th person can transmit. This is followed by KiK_iKi​ **unique** integers: f(i,1),f(i,2),…,f(i,Ki)f_{(i,1)}, f_{(i,2)}, \ldots, f_{(i, K_i)}f(i,1)​,f(i,2)​,…,f(i,Ki​)​, describing the list of frequencies for the iii-th person.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the **minimum** number of operations to ensure that every pair of people can communicate with each other.
constraints: Sum of NNN over all testcases does not exceed 3⋅1053 \cdot 10^53⋅105
Sum of ∑i=1NKi\sum_{i=1}^{N}K_i∑i=1N​Ki​ over all testcases does not exceed 3⋅1053 \cdot 10^53⋅105

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1: We can transfer frequency 555 from person 333 to person 111. We need only 111 operation in this case.
Test Case 2: We need a minimum of 333 operations. One possible sequence of operations is:

Transfer frequency 555 from person 222 to person 111
Transfer frequency 777 from person 444 to person 333
Transfer frequency 131313 from person 666 to person 555

Test Case 3: It can be shown that it's impossible to ensure that every pair of people can communicate with each other, using any number of operations. So the answer for this case is −1-1−1.
